perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) des ignated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person should recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the c ircumstances of its disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information whether or not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons, whether or not (i) des ignated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person should recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the c ircumstances of its disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that information must be 'not generally known' to be considered confidential, the definition is broadened beyond what is legally protected as a trade secret or confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include information 'whether or not generally known,' which directly contradicts the UTSA's requirement that a trade secret derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable.
perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include information 'whether or not generally known.' This contradicts the typical understanding and legal protection afforded to confidential information or trade secrets, which generally require the information to not be generally known to derive economic value. The perturbation explanation and law explanation both highlight this contradiction, stating that the modified definition undermines the requirement that confidential information must not be generally known. While the provided snippets are missing, the strong consensus between the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, combined with the clear alteration of the original text, indicates a substantial contradiction. By including 'whether or not generally known' the modified text effectively negates a crucial element for information to be considered confidential, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party all documents containing Confidential Information or, at their convenience, may retain or dispose of them as they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'destroy all documents' to allowing the Receiving Party to 'retain or dispose of them as they see fit' weakens the protection of confidential information and could be interpreted as a waiver of trade secret protection, especially if the Receiving Party publicly discloses said information. This conflicts with established legal standards of trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, by allowing the receiving party to 'retain or dispose' of confidential information, undermines the reasonable measures required by the DTSA to protect trade secrets. Specifically, the failure to mandate destruction upon request weakens the trade secret holder's ability to maintain confidentiality.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trade secret protection standards. The original text requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy confidential information upon request. The changed text allows them to 'retain or dispose of them as they see fit,' which directly undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to control and protect its confidential information. This contradicts the legal standards for trade secret protection as it weakens measures to maintain confidentiality. The Law Explanation confirms this weakens trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, while broad, establishes the basis for civil action in trade secret misappropriation, highlighting the legal importance of protecting trade secrets, which is compromised by the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information if (a) the Receiving Party believes it is generally known; (b) the Receiving Party believes it was already in their rightful possession; (c) a third party mentions it, regardless of legal right; (d) the Receiving Party claims independent development; or (e) it is released to any member of the public.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the 'demonstrate that' clause with subjective beliefs and removing the requirement of 'legal right' for third-party disclosure, the exceptions become overly broad and subjective. This undermines the objective standard required to prove an exception to confidentiality, making the NDA practically unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Restatement (First) of Torts § 757, comment b, which outlines the elements necessary to establish a trade secret claim, specifically regarding secrecy and the effort to maintain it. By relying on the subjective 'belief' of the Receiving Party, the clause removes the objective standard required to prove that information was indeed generally known or rightfully possessed, thus weakening the trade secret protection afforded by law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces subjective belief as a standard for determining exceptions to confidentiality, and removes the 'legal right' requirement for third-party disclosure. The original text requires demonstrating certain conditions, establishing an objective standard. The changed text replaces this with subjective belief, making it easier for the Receiving Party to claim an exception. The Perturbation Explanation states that this undermines the objective standard and makes the NDA practically unenforceable. The Law Explanation highlights that relying on the 'belief' of the Receiving Party weakens trade secret protection. While the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the combination of the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation, along with the significant shift from an objective standard to subjective belief, establishes a clear legal contradiction that undermines the enforceability and purpose of the confidentiality agreement, as it now allows the receiving party to essentially bypass the obligations by merely claiming a belief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement. The term of this Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date set f orth below and shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement pertains to all information, regardless of whether it qualifies as Confidential Information, disclosed or made available at any time. This agreement will last 12 months, but may be terminated at any time with verbal consent of either party.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the scope to 'all information, regardless of whether it qualifies as Confidential Information', the agreement becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable, as it attempts to cover information that is not legally protectable. The introduction of 'verbal consent' for termination contradicts standard legal practices which require written notice for contract termination.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, e.g., California Civil Code § 1624
perturbation.law_explanation: The introduction of 'verbal consent' for termination contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which, in many jurisdictions (including California as per the example), requires certain contracts, particularly those not performable within one year, to be in writing. Allowing verbal termination for a 12-month agreement violates this requirement, rendering the termination clause potentially unenforceable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by allowing verbal consent for contract termination, while the original text implies a written agreement with a fixed term. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Code, CIV 1624) states that "An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof" is invalid unless it is in writing. Since the agreement has a 12-month term, it falls under this statute of frauds. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (Wex) reinforces this by stating that the statute of frauds requires certain contracts, including those 'that cannot be completed within one year' to be in writing. The changed text's allowance for verbal termination directly contradicts this legal requirement, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.
